Hi, now we're going to go to step number five, which is bullet. This is where we go after we've done our features and benefits lists. Now bullets are the fusing of features and benefits. It is what we call the one-two punch of emotionally satisfying practical use, the features and the more obvious benefits, with a little tweak of shock and delight, and you'll see what I mean when I give you a bunch of examples of bullets that I've written, plus the occasional direct assault on common sense. Now that's a mouthful and it will start to become evident to you when you see how it's put into actual use. All the bullets I'm going to be showing you come from lists of features with benefits attached. So just remember, sometimes all it takes is one good bullet. So you want to create many stories, if you can, in your bullets and paint pleasing pictures in his mind. You write brisk, pithy little scripts like small movies, little scenes, and you use action verbs. I believe you've gotten the free report called Power Words, which is a lot of my action verbs that I went through and just called them from a massive stack of pieces that I'd written, some ads and other pieces of copy. And it just gets your mind going. So using action verbs is a lot better than using boring words. Now two things. One is why we call them bullets. Bullets are kind of the essence of a piece. The reason they call it a bullet, it's an old typographical connotation. They would actually use a circle that looked like a bullet hole to announce things. For example, that's a bullet right there next to the word create, and that's a bullet there right next to the word sometimes, the little dot here. And it's called a bullet because it could be an arrow. Sometimes there are little icons of hands doing this. Sometimes there are triangles, there are stars, there are astroces, it doesn't matter. What they are is it's a way to set apart the one little tiny story you're telling within your copy. So your copy can be flowing along with regular paragraph indentation and everything. When you go to bullets, then you have a bullet and then these indented little fragments of stories and things which we'll explain you'll be able to see. So some places call them fascinations. I think Rodale and Agora, which are two of the larger direct mail companies, they call them fascinations. It's all the same thing. We're all a couple of different words leading to the same thing. Now, one of the reasons I bring up action verbs is I want to get back to that. In the free report you have, which is Power Words, a good example I've used before is the word walk. You can say he walked down the street. And just by changing the verb walk to something else, like he bolted down the street, or he ran down the street, or he tore down the street in a fever, in a sweaty fever, or he waltzed down the street, or he floated down the street on a cloud of love. Those change the meaning of what you're saying dramatically. And people get caught up in using words like get too much, G-E-T. You'll say you'll get this pretty soon, and once you get it, you'll get what I'm talking about and be able to get yourself into the situation that you want to get into. And it's just, we way overuse words. When you get to a higher level of copywriting, I never use the same verb, the same action verb or even a regular verb, twice on a manuscript page of copy, which might be a screen page of a website, or it might be an entire email, a short or long email. But I like to move things around. This helps your reader. You can bore your reader to death with really sad sack writing. So merely changing the verbs can juice up what you're saying. And you'll see some examples of this in the bullet examples I'm going to show you. So sometimes a single bullet will close the deal by addressing a hidden objection. Often in the pieces I've written, I've had page after page of bullets, just bullet after bullet after bullet, sometimes then go back into the main message for a little bit, then go back to bullet, bullet, bullet. And the reason I do that is because it depends how long my list of features and how many benefits I have to my features. I want to try to get as many of the important ones in there, the ones that I think are going to sway somebody. And you can never tell beforehand which bullet or bullets are going to do it, which combination of features and benefits are going to sway your prospect into becoming a customer, or for signing up, or take the action that you want them to take. So you want to get as many in there as possible, but they have to earn their place. You can't bore anybody. You can't go off on tangents. But you do want to try to cover all possible angles. You don't know if the bullet is maybe a bullet about the price, or about what a bargain it is, or what a, you know, how Hollywood stars are using this very thing, or how this has been hidden from use for 100 years. Whatever the little points are that you bring up, you never are quite sure which one is just going to trigger that response in your prospect where they say, yep, I'm getting on board. Let's go now. More bullets are better, but they have to be good. This we talked about this a little bit in the last section. When you have complex details, especially if you use technical jargon or slang, whether you're writing to bowlers or engineers or baseball players who have their own language, you want to translate things into plain English. Don't take it for granted that because you know all the hip slang in your particular niche that your reader is going to know it. A lot of times the reason your prospect is going to buy from you is because they want to know more about what's going on. They may feel like an outsider. That's often why people buy. They want to get into the world. They're using you as a conduit to get into it. Whether it's the world of playing baseball, the world of golf, the world of being a slender person in a world full of overweight people, being a better advertiser, anything that involves moving from where they're at to moving someplace else. They may not understand the jargon or the slang. Especially the slang. They may think they know and they may have it wrong. I've encountered people who have been in markets for half their lives and they've been using words completely wrong, especially the slang. Other people just never got the courage up to ask what that meant, what a particular piece of slang meant. Like in bowling, again, a turkey is three strikes in a row. You may have never known that and all the other bowlers you know are laughing about a turkey and you just may not know and you've been embarrassed and you haven't asked. This is an opportunity for you to be that person that I can trust now and you'll say, you'll say, this will help you so much. These will become very common to you. Parenthesy. Isn't it funny the language we use as bowlers for turkey, for example, as three strikes in a row and end it? I often say in plain English, that means. So I'll say something that is in jargon and then in parenthesis I'll say in plain English, that means blah and then I explain it. Very good point. Remember he will use your proof as his proof to convince himself and others. He not only has to remind himself why he's going to become a customer or get involved with you, but he often has to explain it to someone else. Bullets give you a chance to explain it in little tidbits, especially the ones that resonate with him so that he can then say to the doubters, the spouse who is alarmed that he's bought something, the neighbor who's always trying to make him feel like a chump because he gets better deals all the time, or his brother-in-law who just doubts everything he does. So he can say, if he remembers part of the bullets, if you make good, memorable little stories, he can come back with that. And that is both convincing himself and others that he's making the right decision buying from you and that he made the right decision buying from you before and he will stick with you. So let's look at a few. These are bullets that I pulled out of a number of pieces that I've written in a couple of different markets. One of them is the sex market, so we'll have fun with that. But this one, I believe, might be a combination of stuff. So it really doesn't matter what the market is. What matters is how the bullets are connected. So there would be copy, a lot of stuff going on, and then we go into bullets. And there may be a series of these. There may be just a few. Here's some examples. Why you're fortune depends on some basic street savvy and how to get it quickly, even if you've led the most boring life in town. That goes back to the USP thing. Parenthesis, a unique secret to turning your overlooked personality and the way people actually talk to each other into huge piles of cash. This of course is from a marketing piece about learning how to write copy. Very simple tactic to double your profits overnight. An astonishingly easy technique well used by smart businessmen, but ignored by 99% of other marketers. Kind of the one-two thing there. How to close high ticket deals for obscenely large amounts of money, even in an ad where your prospect can't see or hold your product. So I've got a number of bullets in these examples. You can read them yourself. I just want to cover the highlights. Learn the shortcut secrets of instantly playing 99% of all rock, hard rock and new wave tunes out there. Parenthesis, most songs are stunningly simple once you know the pro level tricks. This is from a course in how to learn to raise your game as a guitar player. To go from just being the guy playing alone in your bedroom to being a guy that can actually get on stage and play with other bands. I think all of these are there. Skip years of frustration by learning the one power chord used by most hard rock and metal guitarists. In two minutes, using two fingers, you'll know how to play over 200 rock songs. And the last one, move like lightning through the three stages of guitar mastery from quote no clue at all, unquote, to quote this is easy, unquote, in just a few short sessions. And then through and then on through impress all your friends to the point you scare them straight to ready for the stage. You'll be jamming with veteran musicians before other rookies have even learned their first song. That's just a bullet. There's a little story there. It's a beginning and an end and it's compact and it flows. The amazing lazy businessman's three step shortcut to creating your first world class ad parentheses, how to quickly turn your hidden salesmanship skills into white hot sales copy dot dot dot, even if you flunked English. Now two things I want to cover in here about writing a couple of details. One is the use of parentheses. When I'm doing this, I use things like a regular sentence as me talking to you right now. Then the parentheses almost like you start whispering up for me a good sales conversation happens like two people sitting down in the corner of a room away from the crowd leaning in towards each other, nodding, talking almost in hush voices and really emphasizing certain things that we can use that. So it's the amazing lazy businessman's three step shortcut, blah, blah, blah, how to quickly turn your hidden salesmanship skills into white hot sales copy even if you flunked English. And of course that's the worst case scenario thing tacked on there just like in the USB. How to find the head turning hook that reels customers into your ad like crazy parentheses. I'll show you how to be the one thing your prospect reads today that gets his blood pumping and makes him crave what you're selling. His adrenaline levels won't go down until he sent you money. Now I should tell you that my bullets have been stocked by other riders for decades now. They find my bullets and they use them as templates to write their own bullets. They like to break them down and I learned how to write good bullets by breaking down Gary Benzaviga's bullets. He's one of the great copywriters that I worked briefly with back in the 80s and he was just a master at bullets and I looked at how he did what he did, figured out this kind of one two punch thing where you deliver something, you add something a little different to it and then you try to possibly do something a little outrageous. Make it something that stops the person while they're reading. When you actually get down to writing your sales messages, you don't want it to be a passive behavior on the part of your reader or your listener if you're using video. When people read novels or magazines, they're in a passive state. They're just reading, they're going along and they're in a very relaxed state. When they hit your ad, whether literally in a magazine, whether they hear it, whether they see it online, they see an email from you or they see a banner ad that you have somewhere. You want to be the most exciting thing that's happened to them today. You want to wake them up. You want them to be in a state of active awareness rather than a kind of a sleepy state. So you don't want these, you want these to be jarring in a way. You want them to keep hammering at certain target response points, their emotional hot buttons. Let's see. The one envelope that no one in the history of civilization has never not opened. That was one of my best headlines that I wrote. I had even hardcore experienced A-list copywriters calling me up to beg me to tell them what that was. This is also called a blind bullet. This is what we're concentrating on here, which is the blind bullet means you don't only have the result, the effect, the one envelope that no one in the history of civilization has ever not opened. You don't know what that envelope is. So you would have to get involved, go further, further the sales conversation if you were prospect to be able to find out what that was. So you want that kind of need to be happening in the person. Here's a good example of this. This is in a piece talking about what I'm talking about. How to write a killer headline that stops your intended audience cold. Find the right trigger words that will pinch your reader on the ass and force her to drop everything to read your ad. Now that's both a good bullet and it's what you really want to do with bullets. Here's one from a sex letter I did for one of the largest direct mail operations in the world. The piece mailed for five years. The usual control for direct mail pieces is anywhere from three months to six months under a year, certainly. This piece did so well. It ran for five years. How to hit and it was a sex information piece from a very, very conservative publishing company who went out and interviewed doctors and nurses and sexologists and just kind of delivered the kind of book that you might give your teenager rather than give them that talk when it's time to sit them down or for adults who had never had that talk before. It's the book that answered all of the questions in a very adult civilized way. But then we had to sell the book so I had to inject a little more excitement into this. So here's an example. How to hit all of her long overdue hot buttons at the exact right moment. And finally explore new spots of intense titillation she never suspected she had. How to destroy your guy with the best sex he's ever received. This was to a piece directed specifically to women. He'll be your slave for life, grateful and super eager to return the favor. Let's see. Last one. This was good. This is an example of jarring the reader. Are your sexual needs, quote, normal? The answer may shock the heck out of you. All right. Getting back to a marketing piece. How to create your own perfect product over a short weekend so you can start earning your fortune even if you don't have a real business or anything to sell yet. Getting back to that USP thing. A quicky idiot's guide to consumer psychology. Constantly understand exactly what makes your customer tick and learn how to tickle his consumer hot buttons to the point of frenzy. And finally the mysterious force behind every dollar bill in the universe that can hook you into an almost magical stream of cash flow. Even conservative, prominent and well-known business owners use this spooky tactic. It's the biggest X factor in rapid success completely hidden from most people. So those are good examples of bullets that were in pieces that worked. This is high level copywriting. These are blind bullets again. A non-blind bullet might give you, for example, might say three ways to get more mileage from your car. Number one, make sure you always use premium gasoline. Number two, make sure you never drive over 50 miles an hour against a headwind. Number three, I don't know what number three would be, but you'd be giving them the answers. We like to throw a few of those in. If you have all blind bullets, it's kind of like bludgeoning the reader over the head a little bit. So throw those in once in a while. But for you right now writing, use these. These are the high end examples of bullets. Use these as templates, as targets to shoot for, as ways that you can see how the one-two punch with a little bit of excitement gets in there, a little bit of outrageousness to be able to write your own bullets. Okay, John, bullets. I got some questions for you. You've mentioned different kinds of bullets. Sometimes you'll give an example, but you'll categorize them. So I thought it might be helpful to explain, again, like some of the concepts yet again. So one of them is something you call the blind bullet. Yes. A blind bullet means that you are, it's more titillation. You're not giving the answer to what the bullet is. You're setting up a situation where you possess some information or something and you're talking about the effects that will happen. But me, as the reader reading the bullet or hearing it on a video or encountering it in whatever manner I'm encountering it, I don't know what the answer is. So we call them blind because they don't give away anything. So for me to progress further to find out what you're talking about, I may want it because it's stirring up desire and curiosity, but I need to go a step further with you to be able to find out what the answer is. Okay, as opposed to, I think, like what you call a reveal or a giveaway bullet is our... Yes, would be an example. A reveal bullet would be, as I was talking about with the car gas thing, maybe I'll use another thing, three ways to take better notes in class. One, always use a pencil, not a pen, because you can erase and blah, blah, blah, always... If you fall behind, don't try to catch up, but skip to the next point and keep writing while a person is talking. Number three, find the best note-taking person in class and sit next to them and do some trade-offs. I mean, I just made all that up, but that would be examples of giving them three things they can do or five things they can do or one thing they can do. So that establishes your credibility. You then are the person who, oh, this is great. You've just given me three great ideas to be able to do something. So this is one of the mechanisms that I did deliver, right? Like there's a combination there of... Yes, I want to know more about this, but also in the next bullet are establishing, oh, I can trust you. You clearly have a handle on this. You're raising all the good points. Right. Okay, something else that you... We've talked about it in setting up for this in the USP, even if overcoming worst case scenario and you... A couple of the bullets you went over in the lecture, addressed an objection a person might have. This is a very common question at this point. Well, and I'm... They... Let's say a point they make or a concern they have. They're afraid to raise an objection in the mind of the target customer. So what would you say about when and how or what the concern should be about that? So we will cover this in the section on basic salesmanship. An objection is a conscious or even unconscious reason that the prospect has for not buying or for not getting involved with you. They may think, I don't have enough money to do this. I'm not the right person to be doing this. This isn't for me. This is for someone else. I've tried something like this before and it didn't work, so I'm not going to do it again. I've heard stories about how people that get involved in this type of stuff end up and it's not good. And these are sometimes vague feelings of inferiority or vague feelings of just never having enough money or never having enough of anything or vague feelings that they can't even think about about they aren't up to doing what you're asking them to do. I never buy anything online would be an objection. I never sign up for anything. I never give anybody my email address. The reasons may be I got burned once and got spammed by somebody when I gave my email address or I signed up for something once online and some horrible things happened to my computer. I got malware. All of these are objections. These are reasons why again, either conscious or unconscious that the prospect is using to say no essentially. A good salesman knows that you have to be able to knock off the easy objections, the ones that are conscious right up there, the ones about how easy this is, can I do it if I haven't finished college? Yes, you can. You don't need a lot of money. There's a payment plan that allows even people who are dead broke to afford it over of course the three years. You can return it anytime you want. That's a getting over the idea that you're risking anything. However, the hidden bullets sometimes are, I can't change is a good one. This product say learning how to become a copier or learning how to market stuff requires me to change at some level that I'm uncomfortable with or I refuse to do. That's an objection. A good salesman actually gets into the shoes of the prospect, knows everything about them. Their demographics, how broke they are, how much education they've had, what they've accomplished in life, where they live in the world. You can start to imagine when you're walking in their shoes what it's like to be them, to feel like you don't have enough money to do this, that you don't have the time, that you don't have what it takes. A good salesman will address that. Often even saying, I'll bet you think you don't have the money to handle this. I'm going to show you an easy way that yes, even if you're dead broke right now, you can be able to fund this as you go. And I'll bet you're afraid of change. All of us are afraid of change. I will show you an easy way to make it so incremental and so easy that all the change and transformation will have happened before you even know it. And you'll be enjoying a much better life, more fun, more activities, more money, more everything. And it will be so seamless that you weren't even aware that you were doing it. Because the fear was, change is abrupt and hard and all that stuff. So a good salesman finds those objections, the biggest objections, encounters them. And by countering them, I mean you give them reasons to say, okay, that objection has been answered. I'm not objecting anymore. Bad salesmen try to avoid those hard questions. I know you covered some of the things that you'll talk more about later. The key point we've run into at the bullet point, just at this step of the simple writing system, is people that, well, if I don't bring up an objection, maybe they won't have it themselves. So I think you've made a good case. That's just a non-existent path. They already have the objection. So part of this selling is to overcome that objection, give them the story to tell themselves to, okay, this doesn't apply to that case. Exactly. That's how you don't sell is by ignoring the obvious, which is often the objections. As you were going over the examples in the bullets, you specifically commented on parentheses, when you're writing what you're hearing, it's more of a lean-in, hushed-tones thing. You also use a lot of bolds underlines, caps, italics. So many people, if not most people, take the simple writing system, are going to be using this copy for video. It's a script of the video. Sure. But at least in your code, and I know this will be based on how you believe or you've learned people read this code, what's an underline? What's an italic? So if they're writing scripts for video, when might they do this? So thus it'll be more seamless, it'll translate to audio as well as written. So I write sentences to be spoken aloud, and that is one of the tactics you want. When you're finished and your ad is ready to go, you need to be able to speak it out loud and it should be conversational. It should flow. There should be no jerky parts of it. So this is real conversation that you're writing. And often when I write scripts, I'm trying to help the person who's going to be speaking out the script, or if it's notes to myself, when to have emphasis on words, when to quiet down. And that would be parentheses, be quiet down. Bolding is like shouting, certainly all caps is like shouting, just like in comment sections online. It's if people are writing all caps or essentially shouting. They take the place of hand movements. I use my hands a lot. I know you do. So it's like we can't do that when we're writing. And if you're just listening to audio on a video sales letter, for example, or some kind of video where you're not on there, then you have to use emphasis in the way you're speaking, what words you emphasize. So I use italics to emphasize a word. I use bold to kind of make a bold statement. I use underlines just to once in a while just really make sure that they're not going to miss that particular line. You pick out the essential words. So if you look at my bullets the way I've done it, it is all prethought. It is all I've spent a long time, almost as much time figuring out how to do the various italics or bold or what goes in parentheses and what doesn't, as I did in writing the bullet itself. It's very, very well thought out and it's on purpose. And part of it is visual, but a lot of it is to help the reader kind of know what's going on. So I'm not reading a straight line. He's reading with the little jumps and starts like this as if it was an audio graphic equalizer showing what the emphasis on certain words are. Okay, great. That makes sense. Yeah. Okay. One more question for this section that comes down to using, again, the appropriate language for the appropriate audience. A lot of the examples that you went through were definitely, they were emotional triggers and given the nature of the product, you're trying to get a raise out of somebody. You mentioned them being their passive state. Let's say I'm writing to engineers. The nature of my product is I know my avatar has at least, their analytical brain is here, too. Any suggestions on language? And I'm picking analytical as one example, but any recommendations on when to vary the tone or the language you're based on who you're talking to? I've written to analytical types a lot. Now one of the advantages, as I mentioned before, of being a freelance writer is that I just have to put myself into completely new markets, new situations, and I got to figure things out. So if you're writing to an analytical market and you're not analytical yourself, then you need to find someone you can talk to who is analytical who will give you insight into how the conversations between analytical people happen. And yes, they can be a little different, but they're not a mysterious other species. They are human beings who are using certain words and certain phrases and they like certain things. So maybe they aren't super emotional, but they get excited about other things. It's kind of like watching the Big Bang Theory where they're trying to make fun of nerds and geeks and stuff, but really they get very excited about things. They don't get excited about Monday Night Football. They get excited about Star Wars, or they get excited about a video game or the new Marvel comment coming out. So find the language that they're using. And again, if you are not part of that market, that's not a deal killer because professional writers do that all the time. Find somebody who is who will help you learn the secrets about how to speak to them so that when you write or speak it out in video, it is the same type of conversation that that particular market has. And analytical people are often just as bored by analytical books as everyone else is. They have to do it for their professional lives. And I have gone in and actually done super technical stuff in a way that, and added some juice and some mojo and some fun to it. And it got very good response because they still, analytical engineers, they still laugh. They like a good laugh. They find different things funny, but the stuff they find funny is funny. And or it's interesting or it's, hmm, never thought about that or whatever. So, if you have to go down to an HP office as if you can, go hang out during the lunch break and listen to them talk to each other, do whatever you can to find out how those conversations actually work. It's never going to be so radically different that you feel like you're speaking a completely different language. You may be using different words and you may be using, you may be emphasizing different things, but you're not out of the realm of human experience. And they all want the same thing. Okay, that makes sense. All right, that covers the questions. Remember, as you step into work on your exercise, there are a couple of examples that we pulled from all the other thousands of people or a couple coming up on 2000 people who've been through this training before that have some examples of bullets they put forward, some ones that we think were really good right out of the shoot, some of the ones that were not so good out of the shoot and some in the middle that resulted in some interesting teacher feedback. So, feel free, before you dive in to write your own, take a look at those two because this is an aspect of copywriting where I think examples help a lot even though they have nothing to do with your market at all. Just the structure of language, how you're getting both a feature and a benefit into the same bullet and a feature and a benefit and then an objection. There's a lot of different examples you'll be able to see and then apply those templates for the sake of creating your own copy. Yeah, and I think watching how others had done it, not wrong, but had not done it the right way and were corrected and then got to the right way. So, the little mini journey that some previous students have taken may be very illuminating for you, may help you get over the hump that you're having of getting this down. This is not a totally simple step, it's an essential step and you should be able to get through it fairly quickly. And once you do, I think you'll really be happy with yourself once you get this going because this opens up a whole lot of doors in advertising. Great, all right, thanks a lot. Sure. Okay, now it's time for you to do the exercise. This writes five or six bullets using your list of features and benefits, combine them, turn them into little mini stories. You can borrow heavily from the examples that I've given you. I think a shortcut way for this to go right now, if these are the first bullets that you've written, is to use how-to bullets. So you can use at least three of these five or six bullets, can start with how-to, how-to-do X, how-to-get X results. And then regardless of what market you're in or what service or product you have, just give an explanation of how to do something, of what you offer is going to help me, the prospect, be able to do, how to do something. So start off half the bullets with how-to and just finish that off. And that's a good shortcut. You don't want to get into using how-to in your bullets very often. It's the most common way to start bullets. And most professional writers don't do that very much. But it's only because it's so overused. It's a very, very good way to get into it. You can change it later. A lot of times I will write five how-to bullets, bullets to start with how-to-do this or how-to-get this result. And then I'll change it back. I'll move it around a little bit and take so that I get the how-to out. But it still starts that way. So that's a good place to start. So get into that. And I'll see you on the other side.
